text:
If you are counting Strait of Hormuz barrels, don’t forget to add the bypass pipelines if you want an apples-to-apples comparison to pre-war levels. 

What matters is not SoH flows, but Persian Gulf flows. As long as the bypass pipelines continue to be used (and they are), SoH volumes don’t need to return to pre-war levels for overall PG flows to be back to pre-war levels.

Iran says the Strait of Hormuz is “fully” open to commercial shipping and that large volumes of oil have been transported through the waterway in recent days, according to the semi-official Iranian Students’ News Agency.
Iran-Oman joint statement on the future of the Strait of Hormuz. Note the wording about “services” and “costs” https://t.co/9ml4ulx9eV
On the Strait of Hormuz, note that the U.S. says no “tolls” allowed; Iran and Oman however are discussing “costs” for “services” provided.

@dantannNYC As long as Chinese banks (and a few others in Asia) do, Iran is fine. Let’s see if Indian, Japanese, South Korean and others reject dealing with Iran; my guess is that if it’s legal, they will engage (see Japan buying Russian crude, for example)

“… enabled China to slash crude oil imports by 40% – or 4.6 million barrels per day – between February and May, helping significantly to ease wider pressures in the global market…”

https://t.co/Afdbmcr9rK

The @IEA reckons that UAE reached ~85% of pre-war oil exports in early June (ie, before the MoU was signed) thanks to a combination of bypass pipeline, Fujairah strategic storage and, importantly, dark SoH crossing along the Omani coastline. 

In early June, IEA puts UAE total oil exports (crude and condensates) at 4.3m b/d, up from 1.9m b/d in March. 

The chart below is IEA latest view from the Persian Gulf (June is data up to June 15th), showing the recovery, with UAE playing a key role (and obviously KSA via bypass pipeline).
The IEA view / data is here: https://t.co/Afdbmcr9rK
